46. Post Sales Call Follow Up Tactics 2-15-25.mp4 All right, sweet. So yeah, welcome in everybody. Weekly group call. Hope everybody had a great Valentine's Day. We had a topic from one of our newer members. On what to do after the sales call has happened, but the person did not close. So there's several tactics that we have that we're going to go through here together today. All of which are very valuable and that we found great promise in, in order to increase the quantity of people closing. There's a lot of people who don't even follow up on these types of leads or have any kind of system built for it. So without further ado, we'll jump into what we specifically do. and what we have our clients do. And then, yeah, we'll go from there. So first things first. Just for the circumstances to like place you into where we're talking about here. Number one is the call has occurred. Okay, so the sales call has occurred at this point. And now we're trying to consider. Okay, what do we do if the person did not close? So first thing. Um, Disposition. So we have a list of questions that we ask closers. At the end of every single one of these calls, let me actually share my other screen with you here. That way I can show you what this looks like. So at the end of every single day, we get closers to send me. So we have a type form built that all closers are responsible for filling out. And they answer the questions. Let's go through this one as an example. This one's a closer. First of all, closer's name, and then just these are all the questions that are asked. Did you finish all the time-sensitive follow-ups? Did you update your CRM notes? Did you set— of your calendar for tomorrow, number of new scheduled calls, number of first calls, number of live calls, number of no-shows, number of follow-ups. To be clear, we then ask, and this is one of the most important questions, of course, detailed call outcomes. So you get all the details. And look how detailed this specific closer is. By the way, just want to give a quick shout out. This specific... form right here that you see this was built by Josh's team with wires from strangers. Uh, there's Josh Troy here in the group. We do a lot of our deals with Josh. Uh, whether we do a deal with Josh or not, we make sure to have this same type of, like, essentially set of best practices that we follow on every deal to make sure that we get the right feedback. The worst feedback you can get, and this is very important to understand, the absolute worst feedback you can get from a closing team. is, oh, hey, the people weren't qualified. So as an example, like we had some new members just yesterday. It was Cole and Matthias. And Cole and Matthias, it was actually really funny to listen to because Cole was like defending the sales feedback that he was getting. by talking about the status of the closers, you know? So long story short, Cole helps people raise capital, okay? And one of the places that he was raising capital— and he also sells some real estate development projects— was over here on Fisher Island, which for all those who don't know, it's a very small, tiny little island right off the coast of South Beach. Moral of the story, it's like the richest zip code in the United States or one of the richest zip codes. And he was trying to sell real estate that's like 30 to $50 million per property. And so obviously you need some very quality people to come through the form in order to get those deals closed. And the types of real estate agents that he's pushing the leads to, you know, in his own words, were... some of the most qualified real estate agents in the world. But in reality, they were giving them the worst feedback that any salesperson could ever provide. Which is they weren't qualified. There's literally not a single fucking thing that you can do with the words they weren't qualified, okay? As the marketer and as the business owner getting that type of feedback, there is fuck all that you can do with that information. You need articulate feedback from the closing team. That gives you an opportunity to do something about it, okay. So, as an example, in that same exact uh scenario that I just articulated to you, where again one of our inner circle members, Cole, is getting feedback from the sales team that he's working with that the leads aren't qualified. What the fuck can he do with that? Nothing. Versus if the salespeople give extremely articulate feedback, like these disposition forms. Okay. There's something you can then do about what type of feedback you're getting, okay? So look at this. This is like detailed breakdown per call. You know of what happened on the calls, the information from that person's call, detailed notes from the call. The amount of revenue generated, revenue collected. Main objections from the day. Like this is an example of if a salesperson was to just give you that absolute bullshit whack feedback, oh, they weren't qualified. OK, again, like ask the question in multiple ways, you know, like what were the main objections you came up with today? What were the main challenges, you know, any tech stack issues, you know, the biggest positive from today? Areas of improvement, you know, personal performance rating on a scale of 1 to 10, feedback for management, training reviews today, training needed. This is a proper feedback form from a closer on a day-to-day basis. Okay. That's what you need in order to start making some serious fucking improvements and to take action to actually help your salespeople when they give you feedback on what can be improved. So again, the two different things are dispositions and then daily. Closer and setter feedback, if you have them, is important. And this is the feedback form that we just talked about. So with the dispositions, dispositions to be clear are the closer going in or the setter going in to the CRM on that specific kind. Contact and saying, 'Hey, here's what happened with this lead in particular.' And then you fire off marketing automation relative to the dispositions that you've received. Okay. So let me give you an example, right? You're in your CRM. You know, you got your you got your little contact here and you got the person's name, the phone, the email, you know, and all the other information associated with it. And you have a little custom box. OK. This is the disposition box. The disposition box is essentially a dropdown of reasons. Okay, and all these reasons are essentially initially, it's important to note, like you're going to kind of guess of what the reasons that a call might not have closed are and you're going to build marketing automation around. Okay, that's as simple as it is. The reasons update through time as you get better feedback on what's holding people back from closing on the calls. OK, so I'll give you an example. Pierre, since you asked the question, give me some examples of reasons that. People are currently not closing on calls. Like, what do the closers say? Uh... Fear, objection. We get all types of wacky objections, but it's usually. Um, like I can read you an end of call report right here. I can pull up, um, a card decline on FanBases Depot. He got sketched out when he tried to submit a minute later and then he like left the call. So basically, fear and objection. Okay, anything else? Price objection. Obviously, we get that a lot dealing with the younger audience. Those are the main two, yeah. Okay, cool. So long story short. Do you have any marketing automation built out for those specific objections when they come up on the call? Nope. Okay, so what happens after a call occurs? What's your current system? Bro, pretty much nothing. They get abyssed. Yeah, and see, that's what you don't want to have occur, okay? You want to make sure that, essentially, with this disposition form, all of this is then just leveraged in if this. Then that logic. For marketing automation. Okay? And there's a few important things to note. Like we work in many different businesses. Where... If a lead gets on a call and they don't close, like that lead just fucking dies. You know, like there's just nothing. That happens to that lead after that period of time. You know, like they don't get followed up with the salespeople, act like they don't exist. And this is— this is a few main things to understand about why this occurs. Number one, the current salespeople are maxed out. So, if and this is very important to know if— Leeds die? After sales calls. The currently, the current salespeople are one of two things. They're either maxed out? OK, so this this essentially means. That, you know, they have. Too many calls. Okay. or they have too much to do. or two. You got fat cat closers. They're just lazy sacks of shit. They're not real salespeople. They don't give a fuck about making more money. Again, there's really no in-between. To be clear, when I say they're too maxed out, let me give you some respect, right? If you've got, let's say, five closers and all five of those closers are like literally fully booked on a day-to-day basis. And you have them filling out like these end of day reports like what Josh does that I've shown you as an example in this type form here today. And on top of that, you know, they have lives. You know what I mean? Like they've got things to do. women they date, men they date. potential families, you know, like they just got errands to run. That person's maxed out. I mean... If they have a relatively high show rate, you know, and let's say that they have 60, 70% show rate, right? And they get the eight to 10 calls booked a day. That means they're having anywhere from like five to eight calls a day, give or take the day. And with the rest of that time that they have available to them, I mean, you might be talking like 60-minute increments. They're humans, right? They could be sitting there fucking scrolling on their phone during that time. If they're a great salesperson, they'll use that time to follow up. But like, who are they going to follow up with? They're going to follow up with the people that are hottest, you know, and those could be people from the most recent days that, you know, had said, 'Yeah, you know, I want to buy,' but follow up with me on Wednesday. You know what I mean? And long story short, they're naturally going to bias towards where the money is. And they're not going to go out of their way to try to rekindle conversations with people that they deem as too far gone or dead. OK, so that's where this comes in handy. That's where we have to leverage the, you know, if this, then that logic that creates some contextual marketing automation sequences for these people post follow up. In addition to that, so we have, again, we have two main things that we leverage to fix this whole issue. Okay, first of all, is marketing automation. And second, is this role that we call the cleaner. Okay, the cleaner is one of my favorite roles I've talked about this at length, but allow me real quick to just explain marketing automation first before we continue on. So by marketing automation, I obviously mean email sequences. In addition to that, salesperson reminders. In different CRMs, this is called different things. Most CRMs call this like tasks, where there's some function within the CRM to be able to log. Into like almost like a to-do list of new people that have been assigned to you. And then you also get the opportunity to like refresh who's in what stage. To be clear, this could also be. a very interesting opportunity for you relative to how you have your CRMs built out. So if you have sales pipeline stages, I'm a big fan of... Lead scoring. and putting people who... are engaged with the marketing automation follow-up. back into a specific pipeline stage that says like hot follow-up. So let me give you some examples here on how this looks, okay? In a typical CRM, you got your pipeline stages. And I'll just use a few as an example. Like, let's just say it starts off with the scheduled calls. You have a no-show column. You have a 'like hot follow-up' column. You have closed. You have like lost. You know? The moral of the story is, in this column here, this is usually people who move. From the scheduled column. over to the hot follow-up column if they don't close. And that's manually moved in there by your salesperson. And that's fine. But here's the thing. Like, if your salesperson moves somebody from scheduled, they have the call and they put them into lost. Okay, it is okay to have the marketing automation move them from lost back into hot follow-up. If... Certain criteria are met. So this is where lead scoring comes in. OK, so when you look at lead scoring as an opportunity to move people around into the pipeline dynamically. Let me give you some perspective on how that looks. You could, in theory, say that if somebody opens like three of the email sequences, that follow up after they've had their sales call, and And... They click on different links within those emails to different resources. That they're a hotter lead and obviously still interested in buying. That would therefore make sense to dynamically take that person from whatever column they've been put into. Back into the hot follow-up column. And then you obviously train your salespeople around the fact that you want them following up with the hot follow-up call. And that's what salespeople will typically do. They have some place, and this is very important to understand. They have some place. That's where they're organized. That's what they look at. For who they're going to follow up with when somebody no-shows and they've got some extra time or just when they've got extra time carved out in a day to do follow-up. Okay. Your marketing automation can be what helps aid them in understanding who's hot and who's not. So the main things that you want to look for, and this is very important to note again, there's all kinds of different triggers that you can use. Some of my favorite triggers to use are the link clicks. Okay. So link clicks. And then email opens. And you can dynamically populate, like a score, simply put, in almost all CRMs that moves up and down relative to what a person does or does not do. It's just if this, then that logic. Okay. You can also just keep it simple. Like if you don't feel like building out this part specifically. Or you just don't have the means or the time to be able to build out this part specifically, at the very least, you want to make sure that you do, obviously, some email follow-up. And then have some automated just salesperson reminders to follow up. If your salespeople are not following up, that's where you take them over to the cleaner role, okay? So the cleaner role is essentially a closer within the business. And their soul function. Okay, this person's job. is to follow up with the leads that are no longer being followed up with. That's their sole responsibility. So they take old leads. And old, by the way, and this is very important to note, old can mean, like, they're a fucking week old, you know, like they just had their sales call. And the existing salespeople are too maxed out. They're too busy to be able to follow up. So they just don't. You know, and then the cleaner takes it over. So they take old leads. And they take responsibility for it. Okay, they start like this is essentially a person. That is. geared towards outbound. Like they are the perfect type of salesperson to work the Hydra as an example for like an inbound lead form strategy. They don't give a fuck if a person schedules a call with them or not. Like this is an outbound setter, essentially, that also acts as a closer where, like, they're just trying to rekindle conversations with people. It's typically best to load these people up with a resource doc. What am I doing here? Blow these people up with a like resource and by resource, I essentially mean like engagement bait. This person you give more power to than a normal salesperson like their function is to take the old leads and revive conversation. Okay, so when I say like a resource doc, or engagement bait, what you're trying to do is you're trying to like give this person permission to potentially give some discounts if they close within a certain time frame. You're trying to. Give this person the opportunity to like potentially bundle another product with the main product to make closing them easier. You know, you're trying to give them like some. type of, as an example, like unlisted videos on YouTube that are just fucking banger videos, like very value-dense videos. Um, if fucking Gamma worked like this, would be a great person to send, like, a Gamma doc to that just has a fuckload of information about whatever their specific objection was on the call. Or just general information about the offer, period. The resource stock, when I say it's engagement bait, you want to try to make this person's job easy, simply put. You want to try to increase the probability of response. The cleaner, and this is very important to note, cleaners can make great money. Great money, just like a normal closer, but it's a different type of person. Like it's not the type of closer that just wants a full calendar booked onto their schedule every day. You know, this type of person fucking loves outbound. Some businesses that we work with, the new closers that they bring on, they have worked the cleaner role until they kind of prove themselves in that role. And then it becomes like a promotion to get onto the calendars. In other instances, businesses that do this role a lot more successfully. They just hire a person who naturally has like this type of desire as a close. Does that make sense? Devin asks, can a setter act as a cleaner? Yeah, yeah, absolutely. A setter can act as a cleaner. A cleaner is essentially a setter to be clear. But you can give them permission, obviously, and hopefully they have the skills to be able to close these leads themselves too. You don't want to have to make them. Uh, go out of their way and like, you know, become a setter to like close on to the regular closers' calendars. You could do that if you only have setters. But again, like, I like having a cleaner that's actually capable of closing people too, because then they make more commission. They get paid a lot more because they do a lot more work than the closers that just get scheduled calls all day. Very important. Does anybody have questions on what I've covered here so far? Feel free to unmute yourself and ask away if you do. All right, cool. Throw one in the chat if you're understanding what I'm saying there. Yeah, great. Again, no question. Yeah, Jeremy, sorry, man. I was just thinking about a potential question here. So the offer that we teach is rather complex, right? So we're teaching people how to build a boutique investment bank. And often with that. It's quite a high-level conversation, and so... The... what we've been doing is essentially somewhat of a cleaner role with the setters, but their job is just fucking reactivate the leads and get them back on with the closers, because what we found—when setters get into a deep convo with anyone about what we do— fucking just shit the bed, mate. They just don't understand the business model, how everything works eloquently enough to explain it properly. So in this kind of scenario, do you feel having a standard cleaner roll is still very applicable? Perhaps it's worth getting someone training them up on the product for like a month. Then getting them to do the clean roll solely would be a better role and just have them start as a set. Yeah, that makes sense. I don't see anything wrong with that. Cool, tschüss. Yeah, cool. Okay, great. Yeah, so look, the moral of the story is— and this is very important note—just kind of tie these things together real quick. It really all starts with the dispositions, okay? Because sometimes you have... And I want to be really clear when I say this. This is also applicable for affluent demographics and like the general public when you're selling to more of like a poor person or just somebody who's less willing to take risks. Sometimes you have to be aware of what your sales cycle is. And you have to build marketing automation to support your average sales cycle timeframe. And a lot of people, they don't build systems like this. Instead, they just let their closer have a conversation and then they do fuck all to support the process after that. So as an example, like let's say that you had, you know, a seven-day timeline on average for your person to go through and eventually buy. Okay, so here let's say that the call gets scheduled. And then over here, seven days later. We have the close occur where we collect some cash. Okay. Well, again, if we just allow seven days to go by and, like, this is just by default right now with zero marketing automation support. And like, zero content remarketing and, essentially, like, zero intention to have a post-call follow-up sequence that can condense this timeline a little more, you might just by default have a seven-day sales cycle. Because you do all after the call occurs, you can generally condense this time frame down, give or take the demographic that you're selling to, and how much information and due diligence you can provide to them to make the process easier. Okay, some sales cycles are, respectfully, are just the timeframe that they are because some industries have laws that you've just got to respect. Like, as an example, in the raising capital space. In certain instances, you have cool-down periods. Where you can't even take the person's money after they say yes for upwards of three weeks, as an example. You know, so like, by default, you have uh longer sales cycles because of regulations you know, and in other instances, it's like if you're selling to a poor demographic, they might be totally sold on wanting to do it at a certain time of the month. But because a majority of people in that demographic class are paycheck to paycheck, they have to wait until, you know, the middle of the month paychecks to hit. Where they have more of their free money to spend on whatever they want, since they don't generally use a lot of credit, and since their credit scores are typically shit, so they don't generally get approved for funding that well. And in the duration of time from when they technically want to do it to when they actually have an opportunity to buy, if there's no follow up, you know, and the salesperson doesn't have an organized system to remind them to follow up with those individuals. It falls exclusively and solely onto your lead to be the one to tell your salesperson, 'Hey, today's the day I'm ready to buy.' So you want to be. Hyper self-aware of what your actual sales cycle is. That way you build marketing automation and content remarketing systems to support your average sales cycle timeframe. Okay. So as an example, like as soon as somebody schedules a call. And they actually have the call. And again, this can typically be like, okay, so day one, two, three, four, five, six. Okay. So one, two. Three, four. Five. Six and then we got that seventh day over there. So in this, in this sales cycle time frame here, you know, obviously, the average call will typically occur sometime between day two and day three in most instances. OK, so from that perspective, it's like in that same window. From day one to day three, as most of you know, like that's where we're doing the hammer them to try to support the sales process. That's when we have an aggressive amount of emails going out to the people. And in addition to those two things, that's also where we have some manual sales follow-up. Okay. This is like selfie videos. Uh, this is where the salesperson simply put is trying to get in contact, all right. And then the call actually occurs. So let's use the example that the call occurs sometime between day two and day three. And then, after that. It's like, again, just to be clear, this timeframe specifically is what we're talking about on this call, right now. What do you do between days three and day seven, right? This is where you should have contextual emails, and content, and then also some sales follow. This is where it matters. Okay, that's where you take the dispositions. That's where you take these contextual marketing automation sequences. And that's also where you take the content marketing and you make it applicable for that window. So look, not everybody likes to do this, okay? And I'm going to give it to you in layers for what I'm about to say in terms of how most businesses execute this. So when I talk about contextual content marketing. Okay? You can take the people that are in your sales cycle. And you can just lump them all together originally, especially when you're not operating at scale. Because to be frank, you just won't have enough people to be able to pocket into all the different contextual disposition buckets. Until you get to a point at scale where you eventually do. So what most people will do first, as like kind of the first layer action, is they'll just create a general follow-up audience. Of unclosed leads. And they'll do content remarketing to these folks. And it becomes, when I say like, it's a contextual content marketing sequence, well, again, it's not like your standard hammer them sequence that you run at the beginning. It's all based on the objections and, like, the things that actually come up on calls. Additional information, additional resources, like think about the difference here for what Pierre was talking about when you looked at some of his dispositions that people were using. And these are very common, like fear, trust, funding issues, price. You know, one of our members. Let me see if I can actually find this video for us because it was really good. It's essentially like a photo booth business. I believe it's $10K unless he raised his pricing up. And Long story short. He has a pretty general class, like a general working class. Type individual. That in most instances is buying from him. This is Josh for everybody who doesn't know. Now look at this video. Interest rate or whatever it is— the mindset, yeah. Exactly. And I'm like, what's the opportunity cost here? Yeah. I mean, because I, when I sold that same building, I made $200. See what I mean? So it's like, dude, imagine if he was just extremely direct in that to follow up that intro there that he made. Like, oh, dude, a lot of people are always fucking sweating the small things. And then imagine if he immediately tells a story. Of somebody who bought one of his photo booth businesses on funding, how easy it was for them to make the payments from being able to get into business with it and just easily pay down the debt. Maybe like... 15% or like 20% interest or some shit like that, but the monthly payment was easy to stomach and again the business model fully supported the ability to pay down that debt. So that person wouldn't have been able to pocket the difference in the thousands of dollars in cash flow they make per month with that photo business if they hadn't just taken on the debt originally with a high interest rate. You see what I mean? Like if that story at the end would have been different in comparison to him talking about buying a building. Dude, that would be the perfect fucking video. To use to support what I'm sitting here telling you right now, which is after people get off a call, they need to see videos like that that help push them over the fence for whatever they were hesitant about on the call that made them not buy. You know what I mean? So like Pierre is a perfect example. Like you're, are you talking specifically in this case about Brez or like just the other deals that you're doing? Yeah, so long story short, like for everybody who doesn't know, Pierre helps this kid I've never heard of until I talked to Pierre, named Brez. OK, and Brez is like this. By the way, I was at a restaurant the other day, Pierre, in a random way. That I'm I go to this restaurant all the time, the random waiter was like telling me about TJR and Brez and I was like, 'Oh dude, I just heard of this kid' and he was like, 'Oh yeah, dude, the Brez guy like crashed an SVJ and then like crashed a fucking M3 like we all think it's insurance fraud or something like that, you know But anyway, I digress. My point is, is like. Okay, Brez, this person that's selling info products that does like half a million dollars a month is trying to hit a mill. Is selling to essentially like my waiter as an example. And my waiter lives a totally fucking normal life, okay? He has an apartment here in Miami. You know, he has a girlfriend. I'm pretty sure he also has a kid. You know, not a guy, obviously, who takes substantial amount of risks. That's why he's a waiter. Nothing wrong with that. But at the end of the day, this Brez kid and his sales team— how old is this Brez kid, Pierre? 20. Okay. And for everybody who's never seen this kid, like, let's just give some perspective of like what he looks like. Okay. So you go to YouTube. You type in, what's his, is it Brez? Brez Scales, yeah. Yeah, okay, there we go. Okay, so this is this kid, okay? So long story short. You see, uh... a bunch of random videos like this okay where it's just like lifestyle vlogs pretty much and the kids selling people on agency stuff pierre Yeah, pretty much. So essentially teaching people how to start agencies, okay? And. remember like there might be somebody like my waiter who comes on to the call That's like, dude. I want to change my life. Like, I want to live like this fucking 20 year old who's absolutely shitting on me, you know, like, what do I need to do? And you tell the guy, how much does it cost? Four grand. You tell the guy he's got to fucking dish out four grand. Probably who knows how much more than the guy's rent. That's probably what the kid makes plus or minus in a month. You know, maybe he makes like a couple grand more on a good month. He probably has like, you know, Capital One fucking secured credit cards. His fucking biggest credit line is probably less than $4,000 would be my bet. You know, and then you got to tell a kid like. Dude, you need to go into debt, you know, like you need to take on a funding loan here in order to stomach this 4K. It's like really think of all the things that that waiter then has to consider. There's some surface level things that are immediately available for us to think with. Number one, just like what we talked about with the Josh video, we have interest rates to consider. Like everybody on earth is afraid of high interest rates. That just is what it is. That's a great example of something to address. Two, the logic of going into debt, okay? It's not necessarily the debt. It's more so the You're making the person question everything about themselves in that decision. Do they believe in themselves enough to be able to do things that they need to do and take the risks they need to take to make it work to afford the debt? Okay, that becomes like a real question. Obviously, the question of what's the probability your actually works for me comes up. Okay, so being able to show people examples of people making it work that are like them. But I want to be really clear with that second one that I talked about. Okay, because these are ones that are not generally attacked. The reasons that the individual you're doing the deal with could make it fail. Have to be addressed. So, like when we go into a deal as an agency, as an example, We tell people, okay, you got to dish out 15K on us. And in the first 30 days, you're going to dish out $50,000 on ad spend as a test budget. And that's the smallest that we're going to allow you to spend. That way we have an extremely high probability of making it work. And by the way, it still might not work because here's the thing. Like, let's use the example. The client's going to use their sales team and we're running a call funnel. What if they don't really believe in their sales team? Are they going to do the deal? Are they going to risk that $65,000 in that example? What do you guys think? You know, so it's like if I leave that problem unaddressed. Is that deal gonna get done? No, but, like, if I talk about, you know, just if I show them a bunch of examples, like let's say I show them all the different massive fucking businesses we've worked with that we've made millions of dollars for, okay? What if I just sit there and start flexing deals like that on them? Is that going to get the deal done? Yeah, probably not. You know why? Because they're not thinking about that. They're thinking about my sales team sucks. I don't believe in my capacity to deliver on my end of the deal. You see? So again, if those things are left unaddressed, let's talk about the waiter again, right? Like Brez's system might work fine. And again, I'm not endorsing that. I have no idea who the fuck Brez is and whether it actually works or not. But I want to be clear in saying, like, hopefully it works fine, right? And hopefully everybody that he puts through it gets great results. But if the person who's going to take the risk doesn't believe in themselves. You know, like they're not going to buy that shit. They're not going to go into debt over that. They're not going to do the deal. Sıra bitti. So it's like we have to also make content marketing and emails that support that half of the deal. right, that talk about those different things. You see? We have to be able to solve for the unspoken things that might not have come up on the call. And that's the point I'm trying to make. What are the unspoken things? That we can help address— in genuine, believable ways. That's the key. That's the difference. Okay. It's not just the things that came up in the call. It's the things that were left unspoken. And again, we have to use essentially like a follow-up hammer them strategy. And this is where a lot of people get lazy. This is why I brought this up, by the way. Like, just look, a lot of people just don't do this. So they'll just roll out. And I want to be fair in saying, like some of your hammer them sequences, are built well enough to also support your post call content sequence. I'm talking about. So, if that's the case, you're not being lazy. You're just being sensible. But a lot of the times, there are. There are different things that come up after the call. That can now be addressed post-call. That makes sense to create, like, a totally separate hammer them sequence for specific post-call content marketing. Okay, so again, it's the same thing for the emails. It's the same thing for the content. It's the same thing for the salesperson follow-up. So. We have to create consumables. That are also for these like post-call. Issues okay, so these are videos both short and long. These are, as an example, these could be like blogs, like written, essentially. These could be YouTube. But again, if and when Gamma gets its shit together, I just want to future-proof this video a little bit because I really like the idea of what Gamma is and what it does. Okay, this could be like selfie videos. Let me give you a perfect example here. Who here joined into this product? I sent you a selfie video if you were on the fence. Was the video like a make-or-break-it thing or just, you know, it didn't really matter or what? Push me over the line for sure. Yeah, so that's exactly what I'm saying. Like, did I have to do that with all of you? As you can see, clearly not, you know, but did I have to do that with three of you and that made all the difference? Yeah, absolutely. You know, like it makes a difference. So again, just selfie videos. And this is important to note, like. A perfect example, Pierre, like if you had Brez fucking pull out his phone and literally just make like a 30-second video and send it to somebody, that can make or break a deal, dude. You know, sometimes they just need to hear from the person selling to them and give them some confidence. Like when I made those videos for Harlan, Timothy, and Andrew in those examples. I didn't just make them a random video, you know? Like I talked to Leo and I was like, Leo, like tell me what's going on with these guys. Like, why are they on the fence? It's very clear that we can help them. Like, what the fuck is holding them back? And then Leo tells me, like, 'Oh yeah,' so this is what's going on with this guy. Here's like a lot of information about their specific situation that they shared with me. Yeah, I mean, that's it. And then I just, I'm like, 'All right, great.' I pull out my phone, make a fucking video. And I tell them exactly what their situation is and how we can help relative to their situation. I try to give them the confidence that I know we have in helping them. Into them being able to pull the trigger confidently. You know, that's all it is. So I even remember, like, Andrew's a perfectly one that I like still today remember. Leo told me, 'Hey, Got this guy named Andrew. He's got this real estate offer.' Obviously, we can help make a fuckload of money like we always crush it with real estate offers. And it's also one I haven't heard of before, Selger Living Homes. And moral of the story, the guy is... Just saying he needs closers. Like that seems to be the main thing that's holding him back. It's just, he's just kind of sucking with getting closers. And I was like, really, that's it? And he's like, yeah. And I was like, okay. So yeah, I pull out my phone. I go on my balcony. I shoot Andrew a video. I'm like, Andrew. First of all, it's like one of the easiest things to solve, you know, like as soon as you join in, dude, I'll help you get closer straight away. And uh, I have like many systems that we use that will help you get closers, and you know, I can literally just post on my story and like help you get some closers. But like, you know, beyond that, there's plenty of things that we can teach and talk to you about too that I know are going to help you and make a big impact on your revenue. But dude, if that's like the immediate thing that's holding you back, like let me just give you some certainty that will absolutely be able to solve that problem. Like Day one, you know, Andrew, did I hold up to my word on that? How fast do I solve that problem? Literally like 24 or 48 hours. Yeah, boom. So it's like, moral of the story is like, 'Dude, the selfie videos are great, but this all falls under the consumables category— is my point. These are... essentially like a resource that contains information. And this is also important to note. The main... And this is very important to understand. The main... Driver. of a decision. after the call. is context. Okay. Context. It's like... Harlan, when I was making the video for you, and you mentioned it's the reason that Lewis pulled the trigger. What did I say in the video that made you feel confident? Well, you're mentioning some things around the other kind of guys that you've helped in the capital raising niche, the kind of results that they've got, why you think you can actually help us do the same. Yeah, boom. We can absolutely help with that. And Lewis was like, 'Yeah, fuck it, let's go.' Yeah. And you see, now imagine if I lacked that context of what they actually gave a fuck about. You know, like imagine if I just sent Harlan and Lewis like a more generic video where it was like, 'Hey, what's going on, Harlan and Lewis?' Like, you know, excited you guys are going through our process. You know, we can help guys like you. Um, if there's anything told you back, you know, let us know. Like, we're happy to address it. Um, Super excited to welcome you in. Talk soon. It's like, that's fucking whack. Like, that has no value to them. You know what I mean? It's like, it doesn't mean anything versus when I actually hit them up and I straight up say, like. Harlan Lewis, you want to raise capital? Like, I got a client literally right now. We just raised 18 mil for last month off the Hydra ad strategy. Like, we, we live and breathe this shit. You know, it's easy. It's my favorite niche right now. I'm pretty sure I literally remember telling him that in the video. I was like, this is literally like one of my favorite niches to be a part of. Like, it's so easy to target the fucking 8 million accredited investors there are like. Rich people are the easiest people to target on these platforms. And they're also the least targeted and the least effectively communicated to, you know, like this is a fucking layup deal. Pull the trigger. This is going to be an easy ROAS for you guys. You know, it's like. that context is what drives the decision and that's the thing. Every single one of these. Like, you don't just want to send a random fucking video. You know, like if you're going to pull a video from YouTube or if you're going to pull, like, an unlisted vid or a Wistia vid or fucking whatever. It should be something that's specific to, like, what they talked to you about on the call that they care about. OK, like again, like for my waiter, imagine if Pierre has some marketing automation or just his salespeople aren't fucking retards and they have a video testimonial from a waiter who became an agency owner. And the salesperson representing Brez in that example literally goes and pulls the testimonial. From the waiter. and sends it over to the guy. It doesn't have to be custom. It has to be contextual. Okay, same thing for like blogs, dude. Like we have the jeremyhaines. com website now. And it's just fucking littered with blogs. And the blogs are all just written forms of my YouTube videos. Okay, like it's not tough to look at. And determine like where we get the content from, you know? So you go to the blog. And it's like, Every one of these, dude. So it's like we can use this one as a great example where it's like, hey, you're struggling to get people like we get people all the time. Hey, my BSL kind of sucks ass. I'm getting a really low play rate and I don't know what to do. It's like, great, dude. Well, we have a fucking video on the DSL. We'll send the DSL SLP to somebody before the fucking, before they pull the trigger, you know? We'll send a blog on the whole fucking DSL. And then they read something like that and they're like, 'Yeah, I mean, this is exactly what the fuck I needed to hear. You know, it's exactly what I needed to see. Or some people talk to us about... Like, oh, I'm really struggling after I get people on the phone to trust me. It's like, all right, sick, dude. You need to create trust assets. Like trust assets. Are the perfect fucking thing to do when you're getting people to struggle with trusting the business, you know? And we've talked about this at length in things like the mastermind is an example. Where I've said, like, all right, imagine a very short video. Where you just straight up go through. And you share information with people on your dispute rate, you know, and like your refund rate. Like that's an example of a trust asset. If I was in the raising capital space and I did a fucking video breakdown on current projects that we've executed and investor amount invested and investor amount returned and the timeframe that it was returned. If I did a video on like the bad deals we've done, you know, and like what went wrong on the deals, it's like, dude, that'd still be a fucking banger of a trust asset. You know? Anyway, my point is, the whole... thing. That makes consumables and the post-call process more effective. Is context. Okay, that's what makes it work. If you talk to them about things they do not give a damn about, they will not pull the trigger. Does that make sense to everybody? Okay, cool. I want to be really direct in saying: You deploy. Contextual consumables. Based on the dispositions. That the closers enter into the CRM after a call. Otherwise, you're just fucking chumming the waters with random contextual consumables, not knowing what's going to hit. Which is also fun. But again, just remember, it's much more effective. Like, imagine Harlan as an example. If I sent you like six different. uh, consumables that weren't contextual, but then the seventh one eventually was contextual. You know, it's like, would you have paid attention to the seventh if the first six weren't? You know, maybe, but maybe not. You see what I'm saying? So it's like, that's my point. The disposition allows you to immediately be contextual. right. It's okay to chum the waters with random contextual things. But far better if you can immediately give a contextual consumable based on the disposition. Does that make sense? Jeremy, can I ask a question on that? Yeah, yeah, of course. On that point, so is it a delicate balance between... Like, yeah, obviously we could set up automations if somebody is worried about the price, but it seems like it's a balance between auto-automation, but also some manual work as well. Is that, is that fair to say? Yeah, fuck yeah, it is. Because that's the thing. It's like your salespeople obviously play a huge part in being able to look through everything available to them and get it over to the person. So it's like a combination of both. Got it. Okay. Yeah, cool. Okay. Anyone else got questions on any of this? Comments you want to make? Value you want to add? Anyone? Go on, one. Harlan, you want to say out loud whatever you're saying in the chat there? I most certainly can, sir. So I was just going to say, like, something that we found really helpful with closers, because getting all of the admin done from closers, correct me if I'm wrong, everybody else, but closers are fucking lazy and they often don't do it. Yeah. Massive ball leg when you're trying to actually grow a fucking company. So something that we've built is we have a system whereby it tracks the percentage of admin done on time. So they have a 24-hour grace period. And then, after that, if we notice, let's say at the end of any given month, they only completed 90% of their admin on time. You're only getting paid 90% of your fucking commission because you didn't do your job properly. And I'm telling you, when you have that, they do their fucking admin on time every single time. Yeah. Yes, Mark. Some kind of incentive-based system helps a ton. That makes sense. We have both, brother. We have insane bonus structures and obviously that on the other side, Karen's too. Yeah, smart. Yeah, good idea. Very smart play. All right, cool. Anybody else got anything? Yeah, so just to reiterate. Dispositions are like the key to this whole thing working because the salespeople know what happened on the call. You have no idea, as the marketer, what happened on the call. You're just fucking guessing. As soon as you get a disposition, at scale, you can create custom marketing automation to fire off those people into different audiences on your ad platforms. That follows up with them with specific contextual hammer them sequences. Until then, you're putting everybody into a general follow-up hammer them sequence, which is still fine to do. But! Again, it's just kind of chumming the waters with a bunch of random reasons. That somebody might buy or might not buy, and you're trying to address those things. Again, the marketing automation, though, regardless of the size of the audience, that doesn't matter. You can immediately create emails that are contextual to the disposition right away. So keep that in mind too. And then also remember, this is a pro tip. I don't want this to go undermined. The lead scoring. So based on email opens and based on link clicks within those emails to the different consumables, that should increase the person's lead score. And then, based on a particular lead score or just based on, you can even just trigger it based on the link being clicked in the email to immediately put that person back into the hot follow-up category for your salespeople to be able to see at the top of that list. And follow up with. I also just want to pepper this in because I didn't mention this. The salespeople should be aware of that, by the way. They should be aware of what brings the person back into the hot follow-up process when they see that person's name show up again. They should be told, like, 'Hey, by the way, this person's back in here because they opened up X amount of emails or they clicked on X amount of links within those emails You know, so again, when they're sold, they're hot. To me and what I've seen over the years, that makes a massive difference to how they treat the lead. Otherwise, they'll just fucking move them out of that pipeline stage completely. Again, contextual consumables are what make the difference. And about half of this is automated, and the other half is like your salesperson also needs to be trained and capable of helping the person get a contextual consumable relative to what they talked about in the conversation. So you don't want to have like an unorganized set of resources and consumables that are just spread all over the place. You should essentially have a consumables dock. That is well organized. For the different reasons that somebody might not buy, that are coming up on calls that are just all within one giant sheet as an example. That way your salesperson can go in and just grab some shit out of there and send it over to the person in their follow-up process. Does that make sense? Okay, last thing I'll close out with on this. Here's what I found. Almost every time, not even sometimes, like almost every time. When we're dealing with sales teams. So. When we came into a business recently, they were at around 700 camera. And when we started working with them, one of the first things we do is we get on a call with their sales team. And we just start looking at the recent people who bought and when they originated as a lead. And we ask the sales team first. We say: 'What do you think your current sales timeline is for somebody buy? How long is the current sales cycle? And they always have some fucking made up number. It is insane how few people actually know it. OK, that is one thing I want to make really clear. Almost all businesses guess and they don't actually know because they don't do this exercise. We immediately execute the exercise after asking them that question.' Hey, how long is your average sales cycle? Oh, I think it's like seven days. All right, let's go look. Then you have them pull up the recent people who bought. And you have them share, just like on a Zoom call right now, share the person's email or their name in the chat. You go look them up in the CRM and you find when they originated in the funnel. And you reply. Like you see here in the chat right now, how, for example, Andrew said something and I just replied to it. It's like, what I want to do is I want to have the salesperson, ideally the sales manager, and I want all the salespeople to be there. I want the salesperson to be like, 'Here's the person's email.' And then I want to respond and say, 'Here's when they originated.' And I want to show. How long actually passed? From the origination date to the close date. Okay, because here's what commonly happens. The salespeople have made up a fucking number and the business owners therefore have also just believed that that's the number. Where they're like, 'Oh, on average, our sales cycle is seven days.' And then in actuality, dude, the sales cycle is like a buy or die. It's like all their sales are like three days. And there's zero fucking leads that get closed beyond essentially three days. And what that represents, again, is a buy or die scenario where leads are getting scheduled onto their calendars. And then, fuck all happens after the call occurs. Okay, there's like— maybe sub 10% of their leads that will be closed beyond the whatever the average ends up being. And then, so what you expose by doing this exercise. As you expose how fucked— This exact process we just talked about is, and then you fix it and you get everybody bought into fixing it too. Because, like I said, the marketer's job is only half the job. And the marketer can't even do the job effectively unless the salespeople are sold on dispositions and manual follow-up to help close people beyond whatever the current average is. Does that make sense? Yeah. I love that exercise but it enrages me every time because they're always so convinced on whatever they're talking about. And like, I'm not exaggerating, dude, like literally nine and a half times out of 10, they're so wrong. and then it exposes how they are and then they either get bought into okay let's let's get these dispositions let's let's do these end of day reports properly let's you know get this resource doc created or they don't it just stays what it is and you gotta like brute force scale instead you know what i mean So just keep that in mind. Like this, this does lead to a substantially greater amount of revenue on the backside. It maximizes the leads that come in, you know, it lowers cost of acquiring customers dramatically. this half of the fucking follow-up is in place. It reduces the volume of buyers a lot when they are doing buy or die lead strategies. You know what I mean? You know, okay. Again, just final opportunity. Anybody got any questions or comments?
